title: Two-mode collapse and revival of quantum coherent state in a tilted optical lattice
authors: Chi-Kin Lai, Shengjie Jin, Yuanzhe Hu, Zhongshu Hu, Fansu Wei, Congwen Li, Tianwei Zhou, Hepeng Yao, Xiaoji Zhou
abstract: Collective dynamics is an important out-of-equilibrium feature of quantum coherent states and usually reflects the intrinsic properties of the state. Collapse and revival (CR) dynamics of phase coherence is a well-known example for bosonic coherent states, which is usually induced by applying a quench. Previous studies have shown that the CR frequency is governed solely by interactions, even in the presence of a tilt quench. However, whether such interaction-dominated oscillation is a universal feature remains unknown. In this work, we show that an ensemble of one-dimensional bosons can undergo two-mode CR, with frequencies set by both the interaction and the tilt, particularly when the tilt is weaker than the interaction. The newly discovered tilt mode is enabled by tunneling between lattice sites. When the two modes coexist, the amplitudes of both modes exhibit universal linear scaling for various tilts. These findings clarify the general features of CR dynamics in tilted lattice models and the underlying mechanism, and provide deeper insight into collective dynamics in correlated systems.
summary: This paper investigates the collapse and revival dynamics of quantum coherence in bosons trapped in a tilted optical lattice. The key finding is the emergence of a two-mode oscillation, where the revival frequency depends on both the particle interactions and the external tilt. By combining experiments and theory, the authors reveal universal scaling laws governing this novel collective dynamic feature.
main_problem: The study investigates the collective dynamics, specifically the collapse and revival (CR) of phase coherence, in quantum coherent states of bosons confined in a tilted optical lattice.
main_result: The authors report the first observation of two-mode CR dynamics, showing that the revival frequencies are governed by both inter-site interactions and the external tilt, particularly when the tilt is weaker than the interaction.
method: The research combines experimental measurements using band mapping to determine the coherent fraction with theoretical modeling and numerical simulations (DMRG/TEBD) to analyze the underlying physics.
model_or_system: The system is modeled as one-dimensional (1D) ultracold bosons in an optical lattice subjected to a linear tilt potential, described by a Bose-Hubbard type Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Coherent fraction ($f_c$), its spectrum ($e_{fc}$), and the characteristic collapse and revival periods ($T_r$).
important_parameters: Tunneling strength ($J$), on-site interaction ($U$), and the tilt energy ($E$). The study compares regimes where $U$ dominates versus where $E$ becomes comparable to $U$.
main_assumptions: The analysis often assumes the system can be effectively reduced to 1D tubes, and theoretical derivations rely on perturbation theory and small parameter approximations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the experimental setup, the time evolution of the coherent fraction $f_c$ showing beating patterns, and plots demonstrating the universal linear scaling of the two-mode amplitudes around a critical crossing point.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical model and experimental measurements of CR dynamics to identifying the two distinct modes (U-mode and E-mode). It then develops analytic models and simulations to predict the crossover between these modes and demonstrates the universal linear scaling of their amplitudes.
why_interesting: This work provides deep insight into non-equilibrium collective dynamics in interacting quantum systems, showing how external driving fields (tilt) can couple to intrinsic interaction dynamics, leading to predictable, universal scaling laws.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Experimental setup and measurements of collapse and revival dynamics. (A) Schematic of the experimental system. A 3D cubic optical lattice is formed by three sets of lattice beams: two red-detuned beams (λ = 1064 nm) along the x and z axes, and one blue- detuned beam (λ′ = 760 nm) along the y axis. The probe beam intersects at an angle of 45◦with respect to the x axis in the x-y plane. The 1D bosonic gas is prepared with zero net force. By suddenly reducing the magnetic levitation along the z axis, a tilt quench is introduced, triggering the dynamics, while the lattice depth along the tube is either increased or held constant. Figure 2: Evidence of tilt-mode (E-mode) CR . (A1) Spectra of coherent fraction efc for Vz = 8, 9, and 11 Er with E = 772 Hz. Vz is kept fixed after the quench of E. (A2) CR period Tr as a function of Vz (green squares). (B1) efc for E = 439, 536, and 703 Hz, with Vz = 9 Er (J = 49 Hz, U = 987 Hz) held constant following the quench of tilt. (B2) CR period Tr as a function of tilt E (blue circles). In both A and B, the red and blue dashed lines denote the E and U, respectively. The CR period is extracted by fitting a sum of Gaussian functions (S3), with error bars obtained using the bootstrap method. The white data points indicate the numerical simulation results for Tr, analyzed with the...

Fig. 1B shows the quasi-momentum distribution n(q) mea- sured via the band mapping scheme during a typical CR pro- cess. Initially, a coherent peak appears at q = 0. After some evolution, the atoms dephase and spread nearly uniformly across the first Brillouin zone (BZ), with coherence later re- covering as the system undergoes both CR and BO. The distri- bution is given by n(q) = ncoh(q) + nincoh(q), with ncoh(q) (red shaded area) the coherent (condensed) atoms forming peaks, and nincoh(q) (blue shaded area) the incoherent (non- condensed) atoms forming a plateau. The coherent fraction is then calculated as fc = ´ dq ncoh(q)/N [49, 50], where N is the total atom number (see more details in...

Figure 3: Crossover of two-mode CR amplitudes. (A) Amplitudes of the E-mode (red squares) and U-mode (blue circles) CR as a func- tion of Ji/Ui for E = 772 Hz, Jf = 20 Hz, and Uf = 1105 Hz. Error bars are evaluated using the bootstrap method. The red and blue shaded regions denote the numerically predicted amplitudes of the E and U modes, respectively, with uncertainties obtained using the same fitting procedure as in the experiment. Inset: numerical simulation results for a homogeneous system. The black boxes indi- cate the crossing point at (Ji/Ui)c = 0.08. Figure 4: Universal linear scaling of the two modes. Rescaled amplitudes of the U mode (A) and E mode (B) around the crossing point for various tilt values E. The black dashed lines indicate linear fits to the data. Error bars are obtained using the bootstrap method. Inset: numerical simulation results for a homogeneous system.
